fix(zsh): streaming placeholder not updated when async is enabled (#7443)

* fix: streaming placeholder not updated when async is enabled

When both async and streaming are configured on ZSH, the init command
becomes 'precmd() { source <script> }', which re-sources the script on
every prompt cycle. This unconditionally reset _omp_stream_fd=-1, causing
_omp_cleanup_stream to skip closing the previous cycle's stream fd.

The stale stream's EOF handler would later call _omp_cleanup_stream using
the global _omp_stream_fd (now pointing to the new stream's fd) and kill
the new stream before it could send any updates. The placeholder never
got replaced.

Three fixes:

1. init.go: Skip async sourcing when streaming is also active. Streaming
   provides its own async update mechanism; re-sourcing on every precmd is
   not needed and conflicts with the streaming fd lifecycle.

2. omp.zsh (_omp_stream_fd init): Use \ so a
   re-source does not clobber an in-flight stream fd.

3. omp.zsh (_omp_async_handler): On EOF, close only the specific fd that
   triggered the handler instead of calling _omp_cleanup_stream. This
   prevents any stale handler from closing the current stream's fd even if
   the global variable has diverged.

Fixes #7313

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(zsh): streaming placeholder not updated when async is enabled

When both async and streaming are configured in ZSH, the omp.zsh script
is re-sourced before every prompt cycle via the async init mechanism
(precmd() { source <script> }).  Top-level variable assignments in the
re-sourced script run unconditionally, so _omp_stream_fd was reset to -1
on every prompt cycle even while a live stream was in flight.

This caused two problems:
1. _omp_cleanup_stream skipped cleanup (fd -1 < 0) before starting the
   new stream, leaking the old fd and its ZLE handler.
2. The stale EOF handler later fired, saw _omp_stream_fd pointing at the
   NEW stream, and closed it  so the placeholder was never replaced.

Fix 1: use _omp_stream_fd=\ (ZSH := modifier) so
the fd value survives a re-source, initialising to -1 only when the
variable is genuinely unset (first source at shell startup).

Fix 2: in _omp_async_handler, guard the EOF cleanup with
[[ \ -eq \ ]] so a stale handler from a previous
prompt cycle can never close the current stream's fd.

Reverts the init.go change from the previous commit  changing the
async sourcing mechanism is unnecessary and caused a regression (exit
code 127) when streaming was enabled.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(zsh): correct misleading comment on stream fd initialization

The comment referenced := (ZSH assign-if-unset) but the code uses :-
(use-default-if-unset). Update the comment to accurately describe
what the expansion does.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix: harden API client and correct permission error messages

Fixes discovered during end-to-end testing with fine-grained PATs:

  contain underscores that fail .NET header validation)

  just 404) for Dependabot, Secret Scanning, and Code Scanning checks
- Update all permission error messages to reference correct fine-grained
  PAT permission names alongside classic PAT scopes

ordering, so dist/ never exists when the frontend TypeScript compiler runs.

fix: eliminate unreachable None branches in glob_match

Replace `if let Some(first) = parts.first()` and
`if let Some(last) = parts.last()` with
`.copied().unwrap_or_default()` in `glob_match`.

At those call sites `parts.len() >= 2` is already guaranteed by the
early-return for `parts.len() == 1`, so the `None` arm of each
`if let Some` was structurally unreachable.  LLVM branch coverage
tracked those impossible arms as missed branches, keeping
`security.rs` at 94.12% (2 missed / 34 branches).  Switching to
`unwrap_or_default()` removes the branch points entirely, bringing
the file to 100% branch coverage (0 missed / 30 branches) and the
workspace total from 92.61% → 92.69%.

fix: add has_digits guard to l_strtof (C standard §7.22.1.3) (#120)

l_strtof used a `start` pointer comparison to detect a valid subject
sequence, but that check failed for two cases:

1. Bare exponent with no significand digits (e.g. "e5"): the
   exponent block consumed 'e5', advancing s past start, so the
   final s==start guard was false.  endptr was wrongly set past
   the exponent instead of at the original input.

2. Lone dot with a following exponent (e.g. ".e5"): s advanced
   past '.', no fractional digits were seen, then the exponent
   block consumed 'e5'.  Both the lone-dot case and the
   no-digits-after-dot case were mishandled.

l_strtod already had the correct pattern (has_digits flag, exponent
applies the same approach to l_strtof.

Add 16 tests covering:
- Basic values (.5, 1e3, 1.5e2, -1.5)
- Correct endptr for valid number (stops at non-numeric)
- endptr at start for: bare "e5", lone ".", ".e5", "-.", "nope"
- inf, -inf, nan

commit.message:
fix+feat: l_vsnprintf %s precision safety, %*/%.* dynamic width/precision, %+/% flags (#119)

Three conformance improvements to l_vsnprintf:

1. SAFETY FIX: %s with precision — use l_strnlen instead of l_strlen.
   C11 §7.21.6.1 says a string passed with an explicit precision does not
   need to be null-terminated.  The old code called l_strlen first, reading
   past the end of the buffer.  Now uses l_strnlen(sv, prec) when prec >= 0.

2. %* and %.* — dynamic width and precision from va_arg (C standard §7.21.6.1).
   %*d, %*s, %.*f, %.*s etc. now work correctly.  A negative width argument
   is treated as a '-' flag plus the absolute value (standard-mandated).
   A negative precision argument is treated as if no precision was given.

3. %+ and '% ' sign flags for signed integers (C standard §7.21.6.1 ¶6).
   %+d forces a '+' sign for non-negative values.
   '% d' emits a space prefix for non-negative values.
   '-' still takes priority over both.

Tests added in tests/test_strings.c covering all new cases.

commit.message:
fix: l_strtoul/l_strtoull accept leading minus sign (C99 §7.20.1.4) (#117)

C99 §7.20.1.4 requires strtoul and strtoull to accept an optional minus
sign before the digit string; the converted value is then negated in the
unsigned return type.  The old code only accepted '+', so

    strtoul("-1", NULL, 10)  → 0   (wrong)

and the new code gives:

    strtoul("-1", NULL, 10)  → ULONG_MAX  (correct)

Also fixes l_strtoull with the same logic.

l_strtol and l_strtoll are unaffected — they already handled '-' before
delegating to l_strtoul/l_strtoull.

Tests added: strtoul/strtoull '-1', '-0', and endptr-after-minus-digits
cases.  247/247 tests pass with both gcc and clang.

commit.message:
perf: bitmap lookup for l_strspn, l_strcspn, l_strpbrk (#116)

Replace the O(n*m) inner-loop implementations with a 32-byte (256-bit)
bitmap built once from the accept/reject set in O(m), then scanned in
O(n).  For any string of length n with an accept/reject set of size m
the old code ran O(n*m) character comparisons; the new code runs O(n+m)
operations, with the inner loop reduced to two array reads and a bit
test.

No new allocations — the 32-byte stack bitmap fits in one or two cache
lines.  All existing strspn/strcspn/strpbrk tests continue to pass.

commit.message:
perf: Boyer-Moore-Horspool l_memmem for long needles (#115)

For needles >= 8 bytes, use BMH with a 256-entry skip table built from
the needle. The skip table lets the search window advance by up to
needlelen positions on a bad character at the window's last position,
giving O(n/m) average-case rather than O(n*m) worst-case.

Needles shorter than 8 bytes continue to use the fast first-byte
l_memchr scan + l_memcmp path, which is cheaper for tiny patterns.

Tests added for all BMH paths:
- 10-byte needle (mid-haystack, start, end)
- absent long needle
- needle with repeated last character (stress-tests skip table)
- multiple occurrences (first returned)
- needle length == haystack length

test: extend l_fnmatch tests + fix strtod/strtof infinity endptr (#111)

Testing improvements:
- l_fnmatch: add char-range [a-z], negation [!abc][^0-9], escaped
  wildcards \* \?, multi-star patterns, ? edge cases (empty string,
  mixed ? and *), and empty-pattern vs non-empty-string checks.
- l_strtod / l_atof: add tests for 'infinity' (all 8 chars consumed),
  case variants INF/Infinity/NaN, and endptr at word boundary.
- l_strtof: mirror strtod infinity endptr tests.

Correctness fix (l_strtod + l_strtof):
- Both functions now consume the full 'infinity' / 'INFINITY' token
  (8 chars) when the string starts with 'inf' followed by 'inity',
  matching C standard behaviour. Plain 'inf' (3 chars) still works
  as before — endptr is set to s+3 when no 'inity' suffix follows.

Co-authored-by: github-actions[bot] <41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
feat: add l_base64_encode and l_base64_decode (#110)

Adds standard Base64 encode/decode to l_os.h:
- l_base64_encode: binary → Base64 string with padding
- l_base64_decode: Base64 → binary, skips whitespace,
  accepts both standard (+/) and URL-safe (-_) alphabets

Implementation uses a compile-time lookup table for O(n) decode.
All RFC 4648 test vectors pass; round-trip tested with text and
binary data.

commit.message:
feat: automated unit handling for recipes

Auto-create standard recipe units in Grocy (g, kg, dl, l, ml, tl, rkl,
rs, kpl) with global conversions on first recipe scrape. Recipe
positions now use the correct quantity unit instead of always using the
product's stock unit (Piece).

AI-powered package size detection analyses product names via Gemini to
create product-specific conversions (e.g. 'Maito 1L' → 1 piece = 1 l).
Stock comparison uses these conversions for accurate green/yellow/red
status. Shopping list calculates purchase amounts in pieces.

Frontend shows amount + unit next to ingredient names (e.g. '8 dl')
with colour-only stock status instead of meaningless piece counts.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Initial release: nested repository support preset for Spec Kit

Adds multi-module nested repository support for independent repos and git
submodules during the plan and tasks phases of spec-driven development.

Features:
- Auto-scan for independent .git directories with configurable depth
- Git submodule detection via .gitmodules parsing
- Auto mode that detects which type applies
- .gitignore-aware directory filtering during scan
- Type-aware branch creation tasks (git -C vs git submodule update)
- Configuration via .specify/init-options.json (optional)
- Full core plan/tasks workflow preserved with additive changes only
